127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139

141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. 153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.

156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. 2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2584	
2585	The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
2586	\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback is
2587	``extension of scalars'', and over a single ring extension of scalars is indeed
2588	strong monoidal. But the sheaf-of-modules pullback is defined \emph{abstractly} as
2589	a left adjoint: it has \emph{no} sectionwise value formula and \emph{no} stalkwise
2590	value formula, so one cannot literally attach a sectionwise \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2591	tensorator to \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\). Moreover the doctrinal transfer that Mathlib
2592	ships, \(\mathtt{Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\), produces a \emph{lax}
2593	structure on the \emph{right} adjoint from an oplax structure on the left; the
2594	oplax comparison maps on the left adjoint itself are \emph{not} provided for free.
2595	Consequently the monoidality of \(f^*\) is established here by \emph{local-chart
2596	finality}, not by a global sectionwise tensorator: one exhibits the comparison maps
2597	\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) and \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_Y\)
2598	and proves each is an isomorphism by restricting to affine charts on which the
2599	comparison functor is \(\mathtt{Final}\) --- precisely the charts where the pullback
2600	\emph{is} locally extension of scalars --- and then globalizing ``iso on every chart
2601	\(\Rightarrow\) global iso''. This is the whole reason the general lemma is a separate
2602	result, with its own \emph{two}-phase proof (the cheap unit comparison and the
2603	genuinely new tensor comparison).
2604	
2605	It is worth noting why the monoidality route is the primary one rather than a
2606	detour. Restricting to \emph{flat} structure maps does \emph{not} help: flatness
2607	governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not whether the tensor comparison
2608	\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) is an isomorphism (that comparison is an
2609	isomorphism for \emph{every} morphism, flat or not), so a flat restriction would

\begin{lemma}
[Composition coherence of the pushforward unit comparison]
  \label{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
  Let \(h : Z \to Y\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be composable morphisms of schemes. Write
  \(\mathrm{upu}\) for the pushforward (right-adjoint) unit comparison
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\), the canonical map
  \(\mathcal{O} \to f_*(\mathcal{O})\) comparing the structure sheaf of the target with
  the pushforward of the structure sheaf of the source. Then \(\mathrm{upu}\) satisfies
  the composition coherence
  \[
    \mathrm{upu}(h \mathbin{;} f)
      \;=\;
    \mathrm{upu}\,f \;\mathbin{;}\;
      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathrm{upu}\,h\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
      (\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{hom},
  \]
  where \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f : (h \mathbin{;} f)_* \cong f_* \circ h_*\) is the
  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism of the pushforward. Sectionwise both sides are nothing
  but the functoriality of the structure-sheaf ring maps, so the identity holds on the
  nose after the comparison of sections. This is the right-adjoint half of the unit
  composition coherence, and it is the input consumed by the mate transport that produces
  the pullback-side coherence \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  Both sides are morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules; it suffices to compare them on
  sections over each open. Each comparison \(\mathrm{upu}\) acts on sections as the
  structure-sheaf ring homomorphism induced by the relevant morphism of schemes, and the
  pseudofunctor coherence \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) acts as the identification of
  \((h \mathbin{;} f)\)-pushforward sections with iterated \(f_*\,h_*\)-pushforward
  sections. The composition coherence is therefore the functoriality identity
  \((h \mathbin{;} f)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ f^\sharp\) of the structure-sheaf ring maps,
  which holds definitionally; the two sides agree section by section.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Composition coherence of the pullback unit comparison]
  \label{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
  \uses{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
  Let \(h : Z \to Y\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be composable morphisms of schemes. Write
  \(\mathrm{pbu}\) for the pullback (left-adjoint) unit comparison
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\), the canonical map
  \(f^*\mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}\). Then \(\mathrm{pbu}\) satisfies the composition
  coherence
  \[
    \mathrm{pbu}(h \mathbin{;} f)
      \;=\;
    (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv} \;\mathbin{;}\;
      (\mathtt{pullback}\,h).\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
      \mathrm{pbu}\,h,
  \]
  where \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f : (h \mathbin{;} f)^* \cong h^* \circ f^*\) is the
  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism of the pullback. This is the genuinely-new ingredient
  for \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}: the left-adjoint pullback is defined abstractly, with
  no sectionwise value, so this identity is \emph{not} a sectionwise statement and cannot
  be checked on sections.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
  The pullback \(f^*\) is the left adjoint of the pushforward \(f_*\), and likewise for
  \(h\) and \(h \mathbin{;} f\); the comparison \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) is the adjunction
  \emph{mate} of the pushforward comparison \(\mathrm{upu}\,f\)
  (\cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}) across the pullback--pushforward adjunction,
  and similarly for \(h\) and \(h \mathbin{;} f\). Since the mate correspondence is a
  bijection, the asserted identity follows once both sides are transposed under the
  adjunction \((h \mathbin{;} f)^* \dashv (h \mathbin{;} f)_*\).

  Under this transpose the left-hand side \(\mathrm{pbu}(h \mathbin{;} f)\) becomes the
  pushforward comparison \(\mathrm{upu}(h \mathbin{;} f)\). The right-hand side, a
  composite involving \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv}\) and the mates of
  \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) and \(\mathrm{pbu}\,h\), transposes --- using the conjugation of the
  pseudofunctoriality isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\) on the left adjoints
  corresponds to \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f\) on the right adjoints) and the unit of
  the composite adjunction \((f^* \dashv f_*) \circ (h^* \dashv h_*)\) --- exactly into the
  right-hand side of the pushforward coherence
  \cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}, namely
  \(\mathrm{upu}\,f \mathbin{;} f_*(\mathrm{upu}\,h) \mathbin{;}
  (\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{hom}\). Thus the two transposed sides coincide
  by \cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}, and the original identity follows by the
  injectivity of the transpose.
\end{proof}

  \]
  the unit comparison \(\eta\) of \(f^*\); abbreviate it \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\). Mathlib
  already proves this map is an isomorphism whenever the comparison functor is
  \(\mathtt{Final}\): the instance
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\). This is exactly
  the \(\mathtt{i7}\) step used inside the project's open-immersion isomorphism
  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\), where for an open immersion the comparison
  functor is \(\mathtt{Final}\) outright and \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) is an
  isomorphism on the nose.

  For a \emph{general} morphism \(f\) the comparison functor need not be
  \(\mathtt{Final}\) globally, so the isomorphism is established by reduction to
  local charts. Cover \(Y\) by affine charts; on such a chart \(V \subseteq Y\)
  factor the inclusion \(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} U.\iota\) through
  \(g = f.\mathtt{resLE}\,U\,V\), whose comparison functor
  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,g.\mathtt{base}\) \emph{is} \(\mathtt{Final}\) (via
  \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\)).

  The composition coherence that ties the comparison of a composite to the comparisons
  of its factors is now established: it is the pullback-side identity
  \[
    \mathrm{pbu}(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f)
      \;=\;
    (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,V.\iota\,f).\mathrm{inv} \;\mathbin{;}\;
      (\mathtt{pullback}\,V.\iota).\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
      \mathrm{pbu}\,(V.\iota)
  \]
  of \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}, obtained by adjunction-mate transport from
  the pushforward-side coherence \cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}. What is
  reused from the open-immersion case is therefore only: the per-chart factorization
  \(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} U.\iota\) with \(g = f.\mathtt{resLE}\,U\,V\),
  the \(\mathtt{Final}\)-chart instance
  \(\mathtt{instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\) (which makes
  \(\mathrm{pbu}\) for an open immersion or a \(\mathtt{Final}\)-chart morphism an
  isomorphism), and the project globalizer \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_restrict}\)
  (the same one used in \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).

  The sole remaining step is to assemble these into the global isomorphism. On each
  chart the coherence equation exhibits \(\mathrm{pbu}(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f)\) as a
  composite of three isomorphisms --- the pseudofunctoriality iso
  \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,V.\iota\,f).\mathrm{inv}\), the image under
  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,V.\iota\) of the \(\mathtt{Final}\)-chart isomorphism
  \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) (here \(f\) is the \(\mathtt{Final}\)-chart morphism \(g\) after
  the factorization), and the open-immersion isomorphism \(\mathrm{pbu}\,(V.\iota)\) ---
  hence \(\mathrm{pbu}(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f)\) is itself an isomorphism on every chart.
  Transporting this per-chart isomorphism across
  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) identifies it with the restriction of the
  global comparison, and the globalizer \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_restrict}\) upgrades

  isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).
\end{proof}
